01a_quotes.title: Can AI Improve Marketing Communications for Small Businesses? Field Evidence from Early Adopters

study_design: To recover practices' adoption of generative AI, I train a custom ChatGPT detector to classify whether therapists used ChatGPT to re write a two hundred word description of their practice on a large patient search platform. I then identify the effect of adoption on patient demand in a difference-in-differences analysis.
I take advantage of the introduction of ChatGPT as a quasi-natural experiment to answer these questions.
To identify the effect of practices' LLM adoption on patient demand, I address two empirical challenges.

I conduct a difference-in-differences analysis, where treated practices are those that rewrite their practice description during the sample period using ChatGPT. Control practices also re-write their description, but do so without the aid of an LLM.

This approach allows me to control for time-invariant heterogeneity that drives differences in quantity demanded across practices, and to control for the baseline effect of updating the practice description.
To strengthen the assumption of parallel trends, I balance treatment and control practices on practice and market-level observables using propensity score matching.
I also include time-varying attributes that may shift a practice's demand curve, such as whether they begin offering online teletherapy.
task: Practices must supply a free-form written description about their practice of roughly 200 words, which appears at the top of their profile and in the platform search results.
I define LLM adoption as the use of ChatGPT to write these descriptions and estimate its effect on patient demand, captured by a binary waitlist indicator.
population: I analyze ChatGPT adoption among more than 300,000 private mental health care practices, which list themselves on a patient search platform.
The raw panel spans 21 months from July 2022 through March 2024, and includes observations on 354,412 unique practices.
I focus on 14 months of observations from February 2023 through March 2024, due to a policy change on PsychologyToday.com that affected how prices were displayed in January 2023.
Most therapists on PsychologyToday.com operate small private practices, set their own schedules and prices, and can decide whether to accept insurance or to receive cash payments.
Studies of therapists in private practice find a rate of insurance acceptance around 50% (Bishop et al., 2014; Carlo et al., 2024).
arms: Treated practices are those that rewrite their practice description during the sample period using ChatGPT.
Control practices also re-write their description, but do so without the aid of an LLM.
Using Propensity Score Matching (PSM), I match four control practices to each treated practice resulting in 5310 unique practices in the panel, with control practices downweighted.
outcome_definitions: The platform displays an indicator for whether or not a therapy practice has a waitlist, which appears in both the search results and on the therapist's detail page. All else equal, the presence of a waitlist is consistent with greater quantity demanded for that practice, and I use the waitlist indicator as my measure of demand.

Additional variables I use from the practice page include the hourly price, whether they accept insurance, therapist title, graduation year, specialties (e.g. anxiety), and client preference (e.g., couples).
statistical_results: On average, I find that providers who re-write their practice description using the LLM increase their propensity to have a waitlist by 1.6 percentage points, reflecting a 25% increase over the baseline propensity.
The average share of individuals in the sample who have a waitlist is 0.063, yielding a 25% increase in the probability of having a waitlist from adopting the LLM.
Results from estimating Equation (1) are in Column 1 of Table 2. The average post-treatment effect is a 1.6 percentage point increase in the probability of having a waitlist.
The estimated coefficient on Treat × Post × Inexperienced suggests that relative to experienced therapists, inexperienced therapists had a reduction in the propensity to have a waitlist of 0.0584.
Adding the coefficients together yields a net reduction in waitlist propensity of 0.0338 for inexperienced therapists, slightly over half the baseline average rate.
Experienced therapists increase their prices by 1.90% after adopting the LLM, while inexperienced therapists increase their prices by 10.08%.
By February 2024, 6% of practices that re-write their descriptions that month are predicted to use ChatGPT to do so.
I find that the 2023 cohort is 5 percentage points more likely to adopt ChatGPT than the 1989 cohort, and that the propensity to adopt increases with graduation year as one would expect.
There is no differential effect for inexperienced practices on any of the outcome measures at conventional significance levels.
On average, ChatGPT re-writes exhibit longer sentences and larger words as expected, but also greater positivity and analytic language, and fewer words from the LIWC dictionary.
ChatGPT re-writes are not more authoritative and confident ("clout"), suggesting that the LLM is not making stronger claims on average about the therapists' ability to help.
I find that inexperienced therapists who raise prices account for all of the reduction in demand.
Average nominal prices increase by 8.3% from February 2023 through February 2024, from 144 USD to 156 USD.

Inexperienced practices do face slightly higher zipcode-level adoption rates by the end of the sample compared to experienced practices (2.16% vs 2.04%) but the difference is not significant (p = 0.71).
ambiguity_or_caveats: Note that my choice of prompt constitutes an implicit assumption on how practices themselves use ChatGPT. Alternative prompting strategies are certainly possible—for instance, therapists could prompt ChatGPT to create an entirely new description, iterate back and forth with ChatGPT, or take ChatGPT output and modify it.

While the detector may not catch every instance of ChatGPT adoption, the predicted adoption rates accord with recent survey evidence (Fletcher and Nielsen, 2024) and capture a population of interest that both adopts and adheres to ChatGPT output.
While I observe the month in which practice owners adopt ChatGPT for re-writing product descriptions, I do not observe whether they use ChatGPT for other business purposes that also affect demand.
I do not observe the numbers of patients at different practices, only a coarse measure of demand, so I cannot directly assess the revenue implications of AI adoption.

Moreover, the estimates of demand effects must necessarily come from practices that are already close to their patient capacity. Demand may increase for many adopting practices, but if they are not close to capacity then I will not observe the increase, which would attenuate estimated demand effects.
Conceivably, practices could use the LLM for (1) other types of marketing or (2) business processes like taking notes and designing treatment plans.

With respect to (1), practices in my dataset are small businesses with limited budgets that tend to not market themselves, but they could use ChatGPT to help them re-write content on their private website. In this case, my estimated effect on demand would combine the effect from both sources of re-writes.

With respect to (2), Cheng et al. (2023) suggest that eventually, tools like ChatGPT could be used to help ease administrative burdens in psychiatry, and may be able to perform tasks like summarizing patient notes with clinician oversight.

ChatGPT during the study period is explicitly not compliant with health data security standards, and using ChatGPT for note-taking using patients' protected health information (PHI) is forbidden (Marks and Haupt, 2023).

However, I cannot definitively rule all alternative business uses out, and my results on the demand effects of LLM adoption should therefore be interpreted with caution, combining potentially a number of business effects of ChatGPT beyond improved product descriptions.

There is one significant differential effect for inexperienced therapists out of 117 variables in in Appendix ?? after applying the Bonferonni correction, a slightly positive value for "curiosity" associated words.

01a_quotes.notes: This is an empirical field study using difference-in-differences estimation with propensity score matching. The paper examines ChatGPT adoption among therapy practices on PsychologyToday.com as a quasi-natural experiment following ChatGPT's November 2022 release. The main finding is that AI-written practice descriptions increase patient demand on average (1.6 percentage point increase in waitlist probability, a 25% relative increase), but inexperienced practice owners experience reduced demand due to aggressive price increases following adoption. The paper includes a custom BERT-based classifier trained to detect ChatGPT-written descriptions and provides extensive robustness checks and mechanism analysis. The heterogeneous treatment effect is explained by a behavioral mechanism where inexperienced owners overestimate the quality of AI-generated content, leading to non-profit-maximizing price increases that are later reversed.

01_extraction.title: Can AI Improve Marketing Communications for Small Businesses? Field Evidence from Early Adopters
01_extraction.study_design_description: A difference-in-differences analysis leveraging ChatGPT's introduction as a quasi-natural experiment to identify the effect of LLM adoption on patient demand among mental health care practices. Treated practices rewrote their practice descriptions using ChatGPT during the sample period (February 2023 through March 2024), while control practices rewrote their descriptions without LLM assistance. Propensity score matching was used to balance treatment and control practices on observable characteristics, resulting in 5,310 unique practices in the panel with four control practices matched to each treated practice.
01_extraction.primary_task_description: Practices supply a free-form written description of roughly 200 words about their practice, which appears at the top of their profile and in platform search results. LLM adoption is defined as the use of ChatGPT to write these descriptions.
01_extraction.population_description: More than 300,000 private mental health care practices listing themselves on a patient search platform (PsychologyToday.com). The raw panel spans 21 months from July 2022 through March 2024, including observations on 354,412 unique practices. The analysis focuses on 14 months of observations from February 2023 through March 2024. Most therapists operate small private practices, set their own schedules and prices, and can decide whether to accept insurance or receive cash payments.

01_extraction.notes: This is a field study using difference-in-differences estimation with propensity score matching. The paper leverages ChatGPT's November 2022 release as a quasi-natural experiment. The main finding is that AI-written practice descriptions increase patient demand on average (1.6 percentage point increase in waitlist probability, a 25% relative increase), but inexperienced practice owners experience reduced demand due to aggressive price increases following adoption. The paper includes a custom BERT-based classifier trained to detect ChatGPT-written descriptions. The heterogeneous treatment effect is explained by a behavioral mechanism where inexperienced owners overestimate the quality of AI-generated content, leading to non-profit-maximizing price increases. The author notes several caveats: (1) the choice of prompt constitutes an implicit assumption about how practices use ChatGPT; (2) the detector may not catch every instance of ChatGPT adoption; (3) the paper does not observe whether practices use ChatGPT for other business purposes; (4) the paper does not observe actual patient numbers, only a coarse measure of demand (waitlist indicator); (5) demand effects are attenuated if practices are not close to capacity; (6) practices could use ChatGPT for other marketing or business processes beyond description rewriting. The author also notes that ChatGPT during the study period was not compliant with health data security standards. After applying Bonferroni correction, there is one significant differential effect for inexperienced therapists out of 117 variables tested (a slightly positive value for "curiosity" associated words).

02_method.rationale: The study explicitly uses "difference-in-differences analysis leveraging ChatGPT's introduction as a quasi-natural experiment" with propensity score matching to balance treatment and control groups. This is a clear quasi-experimental identification strategy comparing practices that adopted LLM assistance versus those that did not, without random assignment.

per_outcome.operationalization: Binary indicator (0/1) displayed on platform showing whether a therapy practice has a waitlist, extracted from practice profiles and search results
Hourly rate in USD charged by the therapy practice, as listed on the platform

04_onet.onet_label: Communicating with Supervisors, Peers, or Subordinates
04_onet.rationale: The participants are mental health practitioners writing 'free-form written description[s] of roughly 200 words about their practice' for marketing purposes. This is a narrow communication task—specifically, written self-presentation to prospective patients via a platform profile. While these practitioners' overall occupation would map to various counseling/therapy roles, the study measures productivity on one specific activity: crafting practice descriptions. This is fundamentally a communication task rather than the full scope of therapeutic work, making a work activity mapping more appropriate than an occupation mapping.

alternates.onet_label: Communicating with People Outside the Organization

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: This is a difference-in-differences natural experiment studying therapists who adopted ChatGPT (treatment) vs. those who did not (control). The treatment arm represents practices that used ChatGPT to rewrite their practice descriptions.
llm_extracted.notes: Primary outcome is 'Demand Waitlist' (binary indicator for whether practice has a waitlist). Table 2 Column 1 reports Adopt coefficient of 0.0164** (SE=0.0069). Paper states this represents '1.6 percentage point increase in the probability of having a waitlist' and '25% increase over the baseline propensity' (page 13). Baseline propensity is 0.063 from Table 1. Treatment effect: 0.063 + 0.0164 = 0.0794. This is a difference-in-differences design with matched treatment (n=1062 adopters) and control practices (n=4248 matched controls with 4:1 ratio, but treated as paired in DiD framework). The outcome is a binary waitlist indicator where higher values indicate greater demand.
source_quotes: On average, I find that providers who re-write their practice description using the LLM increase their propensity to have a waitlist by 1.6 percentage points, reflecting a 25% increase over the baseline propensity.
The average share of individuals in the sample who have a waitlist is 0.063

computed.notes: Primary outcome is 'Demand Waitlist' (binary indicator for whether practice has a waitlist). Table 2 Column 1 reports Adopt coefficient of 0.0164** (SE=0.0069). Paper states this represents '1.6 percentage point increase in the probability of having a waitlist' and '25% increase over the baseline propensity' (page 13). Baseline propensity is 0.063 from Table 1. Treatment effect: 0.063 + 0.0164 = 0.0794. This is a difference-in-differences design with matched treatment (n=1062 adopters) and control practices (n=4248 matched controls with 4:1 ratio, but treated as paired in DiD framework). The outcome is a binary waitlist indicator where higher values indicate greater demand. | SDs/SEs missing; variance not computable

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: The paper studies practices that re-write their descriptions with ChatGPT (treatment) versus those that re-write without ChatGPT (control). The treatment arm uses ChatGPT (gpt-3.5-turbo) to re-write practice descriptions.
llm_extracted.notes: The primary outcome is 'Demand Waitlist', a binary indicator for whether a practice has a waitlist. Table 2 Column 1 reports the treatment effect coefficient of 0.0164 (SE=0.0069, p<0.05) from a difference-in-differences regression with practice fixed effects. The baseline waitlist propensity is 0.063 (from Table 1). The paper states this represents a '25% increase in the probability of having a waitlist from adopting the LLM' (page 13). Sample sizes are from the matched sample: 1062 treated practices and 4248 control practices (4:1 matching ratio), yielding 54,588 practice-month observations total (Table 2). The coefficient 0.0164 is the estimated treatment effect on the probability scale. For a binary outcome in a linear probability model with fixed effects, we cannot directly compute Hedges' g from the reported coefficient without individual-level data or additional statistics (means, SDs, or t-statistics for the outcome variable itself). The paper does not report means or SDs of the waitlist indicator by arm, nor a t-statistic for the treatment coefficient that would allow conversion to standardized effect size. The reported p-value is given as p<0.05 (inequality), not an exact value.
source_quotes: The average post-treatment effect is a 1.6 percentage point increase in the probability of having a waitlist. The average share of individuals in the sample who have a waitlist is 0.063, yielding a 25% increase in the probability of having a waitlist from adopting the LLM.
Table 2: Effect of Adoption on Demand. Dependent Variable: Demand Waitlist. Model: (1). Adopt 0.0164** (0.0069). Observations 54,588.
Table 1: Practice-month level summary statistics. All Units: Demand Waitlist Mean 0.06 St. Dev. (0.24).
I match four control practices to each treated practice resulting in 5310 unique practices in the panel, with control practices downweighted
